topics.title: Lists
topics.intro: A list is Python's go-to ordered, mutable, growable sequence — written with square brackets and able to hold items of any type.
summary: A `list` is an ordered, mutable collection written with square brackets: `[1, 2, 3]`. "Ordered" means items keep the position you put them in and are accessed by integer index starting at `0` (with negative indices counting from the end, so `xs[-1]` is the last item). "Mutable" means you can change a list in place after creation — append, insert, remove, or reassign elements — without making a new object. Unlike arrays in many other languages, a Python list is heterogeneous: `[1, "two", 3.0, [4]]` is perfectly valid, because each slot just holds a reference to any object.

Lists carry a rich set of methods. `append(x)` adds one item to the end; `extend(iterable)` adds many; `insert(i, x)` places an item at an index; `pop([i])` removes and returns an item (the last by default); `remove(x)` deletes the first matching value; `sort()` and `reverse()` reorder the list in place and return `None` (use the built-in `sorted(xs)` when you want a new sorted list instead). Slicing — `xs[1:4]`, `xs[::2]`, `xs[::-1]` — extracts sub-lists, and slice assignment can splice elements in or out. Because indexing past the end raises `IndexError`, reach for `len(xs)` and `in` to check bounds and membership safely.

The one gotcha that bites every Python beginner is aliasing. `b = a` does not copy the list — both names point at the same object, so mutating through one is visible through the other. Make a shallow copy with `a.copy()` or `a[:]`, and use `copy.deepcopy` for nested structures. Likewise, never use a mutable list as a default argument (`def f(x, acc=[])`) because that single list is shared across all calls; use `acc=None` and create a fresh list inside. Lists are the right default sequence; reach for a `tuple` when the data is fixed, a `set` for fast membership tests, or `collections.deque` when you need fast appends and pops at both ends.
keyPoints: Lists are ordered, mutable, and heterogeneous; created with `[]` or `list(iterable)`, indexed from `0`, with `-1` as the last item.
Core methods: `append`, `extend`, `insert`, `pop`, `remove`, `index`, `count`; `sort()`/`reverse()` mutate in place and return `None`.
Use `sorted(xs)` for a new sorted list and `xs[::-1]` to get a reversed copy without mutating the original.
Slicing (`xs[a:b:step]`) reads sub-lists; slice assignment can insert or delete ranges in place.
`b = a` aliases the same list — copy with `a[:]`, `a.copy()`, or `copy.deepcopy` for nested data.
Never use a mutable default argument like `def f(acc=[])`; default to `None` and build the list inside the function.

code.title: Creating and mutating lists
code.code:
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]

fruits.append("date")           # add one at the end
fruits.insert(0, "avocado")     # add at index 0
fruits.extend(["elderberry", "fig"])  # add many

print(fruits[0], fruits[-1])    # first and last: avocado fig
print(fruits[1:3])              # slice: ['apple', 'banana']
print(len(fruits))              # 6
print("banana" in fruits)       # True

fruits.remove("apple")          # delete by value
last = fruits.pop()             # remove + return the last item
print(last, fruits)

code.title: Sorting, copying, and the aliasing trap
code.code:
nums = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2]

ordered = sorted(nums)          # new list; original untouched
nums.sort(reverse=True)         # in place; returns None
print(ordered)                  # [1, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 9]
print(nums)                     # [9, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 1]

# aliasing vs copying
a = [1, 2, 3]
b = a                           # SAME list
c = a[:]                        # a real (shallow) copy
b.append(99)
print(a)   # [1, 2, 3, 99]  -> b mutated a!
print(c)   # [1, 2, 3]      -> copy is independent

resources.title: An Introduction to Python — Lists (official tutorial)
Tuples vs. Lists vs. Sets in Python
Difference Between List, Tuple, Set and Dictionary in Python

topics.title: Tuples
topics.intro: A tuple is an ordered, immutable sequence — perfect for fixed records, multiple return values, and as dictionary keys.
summary: A `tuple` is an ordered collection like a list, but immutable: once created it cannot be changed. You write one with parentheses — `(1, 2, 3)` — though it is actually the comma that makes a tuple, so `1, 2, 3` works too, and a single-element tuple needs a trailing comma: `(42,)`. The empty tuple is `()`. Because tuples cannot grow or shrink, they signal intent: this group of values belongs together and should not be modified. That is why they model fixed records well — `point = (3, 4)`, `rgb = (255, 0, 0)` — and why functions returning "multiple values" actually return one tuple that you unpack at the call site.

Tuple unpacking is one of Python's most pleasant features. `x, y = point` binds each element to a name; `a, b = b, a` swaps two variables with no temporary; and the star target `first, *rest = [1, 2, 3, 4]` captures the leftover items into a list (`rest == [2, 3, 4]`). Unpacking drives idiomatic loops too, as in `for index, value in enumerate(items)` and `for key, val in mapping.items()`. Tuples support the same indexing, slicing, iteration, `len`, `in`, `count`, and `index` that sequences share — they simply omit every mutating operation.

Immutability has a concrete payoff: a tuple of hashable items is itself hashable, so it can be used as a dictionary key or a set element where a list cannot. That makes tuples the natural key for composite lookups like `prices[("NYC", "2026")]`. One subtlety: a tuple is only hashable if everything inside it is hashable, so `(1, [2])` will still raise `TypeError` when hashed because it contains a list. When you find yourself writing many fixed-shape tuples and accessing fields by position, consider `collections.namedtuple` or a `@dataclass` for readable, named fields without giving up the lightweight, value-like feel.
keyPoints: Tuples are ordered and immutable; it is the comma that builds them, and a one-element tuple needs a trailing comma: `(x,)`.
Use tuples for fixed records and to return multiple values from a function; the caller unpacks them.
Unpacking is idiomatic: `a, b = b, a`, `first, *rest = seq`, and `for k, v in d.items()`.
A tuple of hashable items is hashable, so it can be a dict key or set member — a list cannot.
Tuples share read-only sequence operations (index, slice, `in`, `count`, `index`) but no mutating methods.
For named fields, graduate to `collections.namedtuple` or a `@dataclass` instead of remembering positions.

code.title: Tuples, unpacking, and multiple return values
code.code:
point = (3, 4)
x, y = point                 # unpacking
print(x, y)                  # 3 4

a, b = 1, 2
a, b = b, a                  # swap without a temp
print(a, b)                  # 2 1

first, *rest = [10, 20, 30, 40]
print(first, rest)           # 10 [20, 30, 40]

def min_max(values):
    return min(values), max(values)   # returns a tuple

lo, hi = min_max([5, 2, 9, 1])
print(lo, hi)                # 1 9

single = (42,)               # trailing comma => tuple, not int
print(type(single))          # <class 'tuple'>

code.title: Tuples as dictionary keys

Python's tuple Data Type: A Deep Dive With Examples
why are Tuples even a thing?

topics.title: Sets
topics.intro: A set is an unordered collection of unique, hashable items with near-instant membership tests and built-in mathematical operations.
summary: A `set` is a mutable, unordered collection of unique elements. Create one with braces — `{1, 2, 3}` — or `set(iterable)`, but note that `{}` is an empty dict, so the empty set is written `set()`. Sets enforce uniqueness automatically: `set([1, 1, 2, 2, 3])` collapses to `{1, 2, 3}`, which makes deduplicating any iterable a one-liner. Because there is no order, sets are not indexed or sliced; you iterate them, test membership, and add or discard elements. Every member must be hashable, so numbers, strings, and tuples are fine but lists and dicts cannot be set members.

The headline feature is speed. Membership testing with `x in s` is on average O(1) because sets are hash tables, versus O(n) for a list where Python must scan element by element. When you repeatedly ask "have I seen this already?" — visited nodes, seen IDs, allowed values — a set is the right tool and the performance difference is dramatic on large data. Mutation is by `add(x)` for one item, `update(iterable)` for many, `discard(x)` (safe if absent) or `remove(x)` (raises `KeyError` if absent), and `pop()` to remove an arbitrary element.

Sets also bring mathematical operations straight into the language. Union is `a | b`, intersection `a & b`, difference `a - b`, and symmetric difference `a ^ b` (items in exactly one set); each has an in-place augmented form (`|=`, `&=`, and so on) and a method version (`a.union(b)`) that accepts any iterable, not just another set. Subset and superset relationships use `<=` and `>=`. When you need an immutable, hashable set — for example a set used as a dictionary key or stored inside another set — use `frozenset`, which supports all the read-only operations but none of the mutating ones.
keyPoints: Sets hold unique, hashable items with no order; build them with `{...}` or `set()` — but `{}` is an empty dict, so use `set()` for an empty set.
`x in s` is average O(1) because a set is a hash table; the same test on a list is O(n).
`set(iterable)` deduplicates anything in one line; iterate sets but don't index or slice them.
Mutate with `add`, `update`, `discard` (safe) and `remove` (raises `KeyError` if missing).
Operators `|` `&` `-` `^` give union, intersection, difference, and symmetric difference, with `|=`-style in-place variants.
Use `frozenset` when you need an immutable, hashable set — e.g. as a dict key or a member of another set.

code.title: Creating sets, deduping, and membership
code.code:
nums = {1, 2, 3, 3, 2}
print(nums)                 # {1, 2, 3} (duplicates dropped)

empty = set()               # NOT {} -- that is an empty dict

# dedupe any iterable in one line, preserving uniqueness
words = ["a", "b", "a", "c", "b"]
unique = set(words)
print(unique)               # {'a', 'b', 'c'}

# fast membership test
allowed = {"GET", "POST", "PUT"}
print("POST" in allowed)    # True (O(1) on average)

nums.add(4)
nums.discard(99)            # no error even though 99 is absent
print(nums)

code.title: Set algebra

resources.title: An In-Depth Guide to Working with Python Sets

topics.title: Dictionaries
topics.intro: A dictionary maps unique keys to values for fast lookup — Python's workhorse for structured, keyed data.
summary: A `dict` is a mapping from unique, hashable keys to arbitrary values, written `{"name": "Ada", "age": 36}`. Keys must be hashable (strings, numbers, tuples), values can be anything. Lookup, insertion, and deletion are average O(1) because a dict is a hash table, which is why dictionaries are everywhere in Python — they back objects' attributes, keyword arguments, JSON, configuration, counting, and grouping. Since Python 3.7 dictionaries are guaranteed to preserve insertion order, so iterating a dict yields keys in the order they were first added.

Read a value with `d[key]`, but indexing a missing key raises `KeyError`. The safer pattern for optional keys is `d.get(key, default)`, which returns the default (or `None`) instead of raising, and `setdefault(key, default)` which inserts the default only if the key is absent. To iterate, use the three view objects: `d.keys()`, `d.values()`, and especially `d.items()` for `for key, value in d.items()`. Membership (`key in d`) tests keys. Merge dictionaries with the `|` operator (`merged = a | b`, Python 3.9+) or update in place with `a |= b` or `a.update(b)`; later keys win on conflict.

Two standard-library tools save real work. `collections.Counter` tallies occurrences (`Counter(words)` gives counts, with `.most_common(n)` ready to go) and `collections.defaultdict` supplies a default value for any missing key so you can append or increment without a guard — `defaultdict(list)` builds a key-to-list grouping in a single loop. Build dicts concisely with a dict comprehension, `{k: v for ...}`, and remember that copying behaves like lists: `b = a` aliases the same dict, so use `a.copy()` for a shallow copy and `copy.deepcopy` for nested data.
keyPoints: Dicts map hashable keys to any values with average O(1) lookup, insert, and delete; written `{k: v, ...}`.
Since Python 3.7, insertion order is guaranteed when you iterate keys, values, or items.
`d[key]` raises `KeyError` if missing — prefer `d.get(key, default)` and `d.setdefault(key, default)` for optional keys.
Iterate with `d.items()` (key + value), `d.keys()`, and `d.values()`; `key in d` tests keys, not values.
Merge with `a | b` / `a |= b` (3.9+) or `a.update(b)`; on a key clash the right-hand/last value wins.
Reach for `collections.Counter` to tally and `collections.defaultdict` to group without missing-key guards.

code.title: Building, reading, and safely accessing a dict
Counting and grouping with collections

resources.title: Python KeyError Exceptions: Causes and Fixes

handsOn.goal: Use a dictionary to count word frequencies in a piece of text, then refactor the manual counting loop into `collections.Counter` and a `defaultdict` grouping.

steps.title: Count words by hand with get()
steps.explanation: Split text into words and tally each one. The `dict.get(word, 0)` pattern returns 0 for words seen for the first time, so you never hit a `KeyError` while building the counts.
steps.python:
text = """
the quick brown fox the lazy dog
the fox jumps the dog sleeps
"""
words = text.lower().split()

counts = {}
for word in words:
    counts[word] = counts.get(word, 0) + 1

print(counts)
print("'the' appears", counts["the"], "times")

steps.title: Find the top words by sorting items()
steps.explanation: Iterate `counts.items()` and sort by the count (the second element of each pair) in descending order. `sorted` with a `key` function returns a new sorted list of `(word, count)` tuples.

steps.title: Replace the loop with Counter
steps.explanation: `collections.Counter` does the exact counting loop for you and ships with `.most_common(n)`, which returns the top items already sorted. This is the idiomatic way to count in Python.
steps.python:
from collections import Counter

counts = Counter(words)
print(counts.most_common(3))
print(counts["missing"])   # 0, not a KeyError

steps.title: Group words by length with defaultdict
steps.explanation: A `defaultdict(list)` auto-creates an empty list the first time you touch a key, so you can append immediately. Here we bucket every word by how many letters it has.
steps.python:
from collections import defaultdict

by_length = defaultdict(list)
for word in set(words):       # set() to dedupe first
    by_length[len(word)].append(word)

for length in sorted(by_length):
    print(length, "->", sorted(by_length[length]))

practice: Modify the manual counter to ignore words shorter than 3 letters before counting.
Use `counts.most_common()` with no argument and print the full ranking; confirm it matches your hand-sorted version.
Write a `defaultdict(int)` version of the word counter and compare it to the `get()` version — which reads more clearly to you?
Group the words by their first letter instead of length, then print each group sorted alphabetically.
Read a real `.txt` file with `open(...).read()`, strip punctuation with `str.translate` or a comprehension, and report the 10 most common words.

topics.title: List Comprehensions
topics.intro: List comprehensions build a new list from an iterable in one readable expression — Python's idiomatic alternative to a build-up loop.
summary: A list comprehension constructs a list in a single expression: `[expr for item in iterable]`. It is the Pythonic replacement for the common pattern of creating an empty list and appending inside a `for` loop — `[x * x for x in range(5)]` produces `[0, 1, 4, 9, 16]` with no `result = []` and no `.append`. You can add a filter with a trailing `if`: `[x for x in nums if x % 2 == 0]` keeps only the even numbers. The mental model is "map and filter, left to right": the leading expression transforms each item, and the optional `if` decides which items survive.

The same bracket-and-clause syntax generalizes to other collections. Swap the brackets for braces to get a set comprehension, `{x % 3 for x in range(10)}`, or a dict comprehension with a `key: value` head, `{w: len(w) for w in words}`. A conditional *expression* can sit in the output position to choose between two values per item — `["even" if x % 2 == 0 else "odd" for x in nums]` — which is different from the trailing filter `if` that decides inclusion. You can also nest loops: `[(r, c) for r in rows for c in cols]` reads top to bottom in the same order you would write the equivalent nested `for` statements.

Comprehensions are about building a collection, not about side effects: never write a comprehension just to call `print` or mutate something — use a plain loop for that. And keep them readable. A comprehension that needs several clauses, deep nesting, or wraps past a comfortable line length is clearer as an explicit loop; the rule of thumb is that if you cannot explain it in one breath, expand it. When the result is large and you only iterate it once, prefer a generator expression (parentheses instead of brackets) so you never materialize the whole list in memory.
keyPoints: Syntax is `[expr for item in iterable if condition]`; the `if` at the end filters, choosing which items are included.
A conditional in the output position (`a if cond else b`) transforms each item and is distinct from the trailing filter `if`.
The same form yields set comprehensions `{...}` and dict comprehensions `{k: v for ...}`.
Nested loops read in statement order: `[... for r in rows for c in cols]` equals the equivalent nested `for`.
Use comprehensions to build collections, never for pure side effects like `print` — use a loop there.
Favor a generator expression (parentheses) over a list comprehension when the result is large and used only once.

code.title: Map, filter, and the conditional expression
code.code:
nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]

squares = [n * n for n in nums]
print(squares)                 # [1, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36]

evens = [n for n in nums if n % 2 == 0]
print(evens)                   # [2, 4, 6]

# conditional EXPRESSION in the output position (not a filter)
labels = ["even" if n % 2 == 0 else "odd" for n in nums]
print(labels)                  # ['odd', 'even', 'odd', 'even', 'odd', 'even']

# the equivalent explicit loop, for comparison
result = []
for n in nums:
    if n % 2 == 0:
        result.append(n * n)
print(result)                  # [4, 16, 36]

code.title: Set, dict, and nested comprehensions
code.code:
words = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]

lengths = {w: len(w) for w in words}      # dict comprehension
print(lengths)            # {'apple': 5, 'banana': 6, 'cherry': 6}

remainders = {n % 3 for n in range(10)}   # set comprehension
print(remainders)         # {0, 1, 2}

# nested loops -> a flat list of coordinate pairs
grid = [(r, c) for r in range(2) for c in range(3)]
print(grid)               # [(0,0),(0,1),(0,2),(1,0),(1,1),(1,2)]

# flatten a 2D list
matrix = [[1, 2], [3, 4], [5, 6]]
flat = [x for row in matrix for x in row]
print(flat)               # [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]

handsOn.goal: Rewrite a series of build-up loops as comprehensions, then practice filtering, conditional expressions, and the set/dict variants.

steps.title: Turn a build-up loop into a comprehension
steps.explanation: Start from the verbose pattern — empty list plus append — and collapse it into one line. Both produce the same list; the comprehension just states the intent more directly.
steps.python:
# verbose version
squares = []
for n in range(1, 11):
    squares.append(n * n)
print(squares)

# the comprehension that replaces it
squares = [n * n for n in range(1, 11)]
print(squares)

steps.title: Add a filter
steps.explanation: A trailing `if` keeps only the items that satisfy a condition. Here we keep numbers divisible by 3, transforming them as we go.
steps.python:
result = [n * n for n in range(1, 21) if n % 3 == 0]
print(result)   # squares of 3, 6, 9, 12, 15, 18

steps.title: Transform each item with a conditional expression
steps.explanation: Put `a if cond else b` in the output position to choose a value per item. This is FizzBuzz-style mapping — every item produces an output, unlike the filter `if` which drops items.
steps.python:
nums = range(1, 11)
fizz = ["fizz" if n % 3 == 0 else n for n in nums]
print(fizz)   # [1, 2, 'fizz', 4, 5, 'fizz', 7, 8, 'fizz', 10]

steps.title: Use the dict and set variants
steps.explanation: Swap brackets for braces to build a set; add a `key: value` head to build a dict. Same comprehension machinery, different output collection.

practice: Build a list of the first 10 powers of two using a comprehension over `range(10)`.
From a list of words, build a comprehension that keeps only words longer than 4 letters, uppercased.
Write a comprehension that produces `["FizzBuzz" if divisible by 15 else "Fizz"/"Buzz"/n]` for 1..30 using nested conditional expressions.
Given `pairs = [("a", 1), ("b", 2)]`, build a dict from it with a comprehension (then confirm `dict(pairs)` does the same).
Flatten a 3-level nested list with a comprehension that has three `for` clauses, and check it against a manual loop.

topics.title: Generator Expressions
topics.intro: A generator expression looks like a list comprehension in parentheses, but produces values lazily one at a time instead of building the whole list in memory.
summary: A generator expression has the exact syntax of a list comprehension with parentheses instead of square brackets: `(x * x for x in range(10))`. The crucial difference is laziness. A list comprehension computes every element immediately and stores the full list; a generator expression returns a generator object that computes each value on demand as you iterate, holding only one value at a time. For a million-element sequence that you only pass through once, this is the difference between allocating a giant list and allocating almost nothing — generators shine on large or infinite streams and on pipelines where data flows through several stages.

Because they are lazy, generators are single-use: once you iterate to the end (with a `for` loop, `sum`, `list`, `max`, and so on) they are exhausted and yield nothing more. You cannot index a generator or call `len` on it, and you cannot iterate it twice — re-create the expression if you need a second pass. They compose beautifully: chaining generator expressions builds a streaming pipeline where each stage pulls one item from the previous, so memory stays flat no matter how long the source is. A particularly clean idiom is dropping the parentheses when a generator is the sole argument to a function: `sum(x * x for x in nums)` needs no extra brackets.

The practical decision rule: reach for a generator expression when you are consuming the result once and want to save memory, and a list comprehension when you need the materialized list — to index it, take its length, iterate it multiple times, or pass it where a real list is required. Generator expressions are the inline cousins of generator functions (which use `yield`); the expression form is perfect for short transformations, while a `def ... yield` function is better when the per-item logic is complex or needs setup. Feeding aggregate functions — `sum`, `max`, `min`, `any`, `all`, `sorted` — directly from a generator expression is among the most idiomatic patterns in modern Python.
keyPoints: Same syntax as a list comprehension but with parentheses: `(expr for item in iterable)` returns a lazy generator.
Values are produced one at a time on demand, so memory stays flat even for huge or infinite sequences.
Generators are single-use and not indexable: you cannot re-iterate, slice, or call `len` on them.
Omit the parentheses when the generator is a function's only argument, e.g. `sum(x*x for x in nums)`.
Choose a list comprehension when you need the full list (indexing, length, multiple passes); a generator when you consume it once.
They chain into streaming pipelines and pair perfectly with `sum`, `max`, `any`, `all`, and `sorted`.

code.title: Generator vs list comprehension
code.code:
import sys

gen = (x * x for x in range(1_000_000))   # lazy: builds nothing yet
lst = [x * x for x in range(1_000_000)]   # eager: builds a huge list

print(type(gen))                  # <class 'generator'>
print(sys.getsizeof(gen))         # ~200 bytes regardless of size
print(sys.getsizeof(lst))         # many megabytes

# consume the generator one item at a time
print(next(gen))                  # 0
print(next(gen))                  # 1

# a generator is single-use: once exhausted, it is empty
squares = (n * n for n in range(3))
print(list(squares))              # [0, 1, 4]
print(list(squares))              # [] -- already consumed

code.title: Streaming aggregation and pipelines
code.code:
nums = range(1, 1_000_001)

# feed a generator straight into sum() -- no extra parentheses needed
total = sum(x * x for x in nums if x % 2 == 0)
print(total)

# any() / all() short-circuit and stop early on a generator
has_big = any(x > 999_000 for x in nums)
print(has_big)                    # True (stops as soon as it finds one)

# chained generators form a memory-flat pipeline
lines = (line.strip() for line in ["  a ", "", " b", "c "])
nonempty = (ln for ln in lines if ln)
upper = (ln.upper() for ln in nonempty)
print(list(upper))                # ['A', 'B', 'C']

handsOn.goal: Measure the memory difference between a list comprehension and a generator expression, then build a streaming pipeline that processes a large sequence without materializing it.

steps.title: See the memory difference
steps.explanation: Build the same squares two ways and compare their sizes with `sys.getsizeof`. The list grows with the data; the generator stays tiny because it stores only the recipe, not the results.
steps.python:
import sys

lst = [x * x for x in range(100_000)]
gen = (x * x for x in range(100_000))

print("list bytes:", sys.getsizeof(lst))   # large
print("gen bytes: ", sys.getsizeof(gen))   # tiny and constant

steps.title: Consume lazily with next()
steps.explanation: A generator yields one value per `next()` call and remembers where it left off. When there is nothing left it raises `StopIteration` — which is exactly what a `for` loop catches for you under the hood.
steps.python:
gen = (x * x for x in range(3))
print(next(gen))   # 0
print(next(gen))   # 1
print(next(gen))   # 4
try:
    next(gen)
except StopIteration:
    print("exhausted")

steps.title: Feed a generator into an aggregate
steps.explanation: Aggregate functions consume an iterator to completion. Passing a generator expression directly to `sum` keeps memory flat even over a million items — and the parentheses are optional when it is the only argument.
steps.python:
total = sum(x for x in range(1_000_000) if x % 7 == 0)
print(total)

biggest = max(len(w) for w in ["hi", "hello", "hey"])
print(biggest)   # 5

steps.title: Chain generators into a pipeline
steps.explanation: Each generator pulls one item from the previous stage, so the whole chain processes the source one element at a time. Nothing is materialized until the final `list()` (or other consumer) pulls it through.
steps.python:
raw = (f"  item-{i}  " for i in range(5))
stripped = (s.strip() for s in raw)
labeled = (s.upper() for s in stripped if int(s.split('-')[1]) % 2 == 0)

print(list(labeled))   # ['ITEM-0', 'ITEM-2', 'ITEM-4']

practice: Replace a list comprehension you wrote earlier with a generator expression inside `sum()` and confirm the result is identical.
Prove a generator is single-use: call `list()` on the same generator twice and observe the second result is empty.
Use `any()` with a generator to check whether any number in `range(1_000_000)` is divisible by 999_983, and reason about why it can stop early.
Build a 3-stage pipeline that reads lines from a file, strips them, and yields only non-empty ones — without ever building a full list.
Compare timing of `sum([...])` vs `sum(...)` over `range(10_000_000)` and explain the memory (not just speed) difference.
